name: Philosophy
description: Great thinkers, schools of thought, and famous ideas

questions.q: Who is widely considered the father of Western philosophy?

source.notes: ~470-399 BC. Teacher of Plato. Left no writings; known through students' work.

questions.q: Whose early dialogues feature Socrates as the main character?

source.notes: The 'Socratic dialogues.' Plato was Socrates's student; Aristotle was Plato's student.

questions.q: Who founded Taoism (Daoism) and is the reputed author of the Tao Te Ching?

source.notes: Also romanized as 'Laozi.' Means 'Old Master.' Semi-legendary 6th century BC figure.

questions.q: Which Chinese philosopher's teachings include the 'Analects' and emphasize virtue and social harmony?

source.notes: Kong Fuzi (551-479 BC). Educator, politician, and moral philosopher.

questions.q: In which modern-day country was Siddhartha Gautama (the Buddha) born?

source.notes: Born in Lumbini, modern-day Nepal, ~563 BC. Founder of Buddhism.

questions.q: Who famously wrote 'I think, therefore I am' (Cogito ergo sum)?

source.notes: French philosopher (1596-1650). Considered the father of modern philosophy.

questions.q: Which German philosopher wrote 'The Communist Manifesto' with Friedrich Engels?

source.notes: Published 1848. Marx also wrote 'Das Kapital.'

questions.q: Who declared 'God is dead'?

source.notes: German philosopher (1844-1900). In 'The Gay Science' (1882) and 'Thus Spoke Zarathustra.'

questions.q: Which Greek philosopher was Plato's student and tutor to Alexander the Great?

source.notes: 384-322 BC. Wrote on politics, ethics, metaphysics, biology. Founded the Lyceum school.

questions.q: What philosophical concept does Jean-Paul Sartre's 'existence precedes essence' represent?

source.notes: We exist first, then define ourselves through actions. Sartre (1905-1980) was French.

questions.q: Who developed the categorical imperative as a moral framework?

source.notes: German philosopher (1724-1804). Act only according to rules you could will to be universal.

questions.q: Which philosophy emphasizes seeking the greatest happiness for the greatest number?

source.notes: Founded by Jeremy Bentham, developed by John Stuart Mill.

questions.q: Who wrote 'The Republic,' featuring the Allegory of the Cave?

source.notes: ~375 BC. Also describes Plato's ideal society and theory of forms.

questions.q: Which ancient Greek philosopher is famous for the Pythagorean theorem?

source.notes: ~570-495 BC. Also founded a religious/philosophical movement in southern Italy.

questions.q: Who wrote 'Meditations,' a work reflecting Stoic philosophy, while serving as Roman emperor?

source.notes: Ruled 161-180 AD. 'Last of the Five Good Emperors.' Wrote for private reflection, not publication.

questions.q: Benedictus (Baruch) Spinoza's masterwork is titled what?

source.notes: Dutch philosopher (1632-1677). Excommunicated from Jewish community for his views. Published posthumously.

questions.q: Which school of Greek philosophy emphasized acceptance of fate and virtuous living regardless of circumstances?

source.notes: Founded by Zeno of Citium (~300 BC). Modern practitioners include Marcus Aurelius and Seneca.

questions.q: Which 18th-century French philosopher wrote 'Candide' and championed free speech and religious tolerance?

source.notes: Pen name of François-Marie Arouet (1694-1778). Key Enlightenment thinker. 'Candide' satirizes optimism.

questions.q: Whose famous work 'The Social Contract' begins with 'Man is born free, and everywhere he is in chains'?

source.notes: Published 1762. Influenced the French Revolution.

questions.q: Who wrote 'Beyond Good and Evil' and developed the concept of the 'Übermensch'?

source.notes: 'Übermensch' sometimes translated 'superman' or 'overman.' Nietzsche's 1886 work.
